cryptogramPlaintext: Man is the measure of all things.
explanation: Protagoras of Abdera, a 5th-century BCE Greek Sophist, is credited with this line, preserved chiefly through Plato's dialogue Theaetetus, where Socrates quotes it to discuss the nature of knowledge. It became the founding slogan of philosophical relativism: the idea that truth is measured against the individual perceiver rather than fixed in the world.

The English philosopher Herbert Spencer, who coined the phrase 'survival of the fittest,' wrote this in his 1861 book Education: Intellectual, Moral, and Physical. The full sentence continues, 'and before knowledge can be organized, some of it must first be possessed,' reflecting his conviction that careful observation must come before theory.

cryptogramPlaintext: Learning without thought is labour lost.
explanation: This comes from the Analects of Confucius, recorded by the disciples of the Chinese sage who lived from 551 to 479 BCE. It is one half of a balanced maxim whose second part warns that 'thought without learning is perilous,' insisting that study and reflection each fail without the other.

cryptogramPlaintext: Nothing in life is to be feared, it is only to be understood.
explanation: Marie Curie, the only person ever to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, is widely credited with this call to replace fear with understanding. A commonly appended second sentence may actually have been added later by the chemist Glenn Seaborg, so this entry uses only the line firmly tied to Curie.

cryptogramPlaintext: Ignorance more frequently begets confidence than does knowledge.
explanation: Charles Darwin wrote this in the introduction to The Descent of Man (1871), rebutting critics who insisted that the origin of humankind could never be discovered by science. He noted that it is those who know little, not those who know much, who declare problems unsolvable, anticipating what psychologists would later name the Dunning-Kruger effect.

cryptogramPlaintext: The first principle is that you must not fool yourself and you are the easiest person to fool.
explanation: The Nobel-winning physicist Richard Feynman delivered this in his 1974 Caltech commencement address, later published as 'Cargo Cult Science,' a meditation on scientific integrity. His point was that honest inquiry begins with relentless self-skepticism, because our own wishes are the bias hardest to detect.

cryptogramPlaintext: The fundamental cause of the trouble is that in the modern world the stupid are cocksure while the intelligent are full of doubt.
explanation: Bertrand Russell wrote this in his essay 'The Triumph of Stupidity,' dated 10 May 1933, as he watched the Nazi movement rise in Germany. The philosopher and mathematician lamented that certainty and competence so often run in opposite directions, leaving the thoughtful hesitant while the reckless press ahead.

cryptogramPlaintext: We are generally more effectually persuaded by reasons we have ourselves discovered than by those which have occurred to others.
explanation: The French mathematician and philosopher Blaise Pascal recorded this in the Pensees, the collection of fragments published in 1670, eight years after his death. He observed that people are moved far more by conclusions they reach for themselves than by arguments simply handed to them, an early insight into how persuasion really works.

cryptogramPlaintext: I do not feel obliged to believe that the same God who has endowed us with sense, reason, and intellect has intended us to forgo their use.
explanation: Galileo wrote this in his 1615 Letter to the Grand Duchess Christina, defending the compatibility of scientific inquiry with scripture as the Church began moving against his support for Copernican astronomy. His argument was that a God who granted humans reason surely meant for them to use it rather than abandon it.

cryptogramPlaintext: I have striven not to laugh at human actions, not to weep at them, nor to hate them, but to understand them.
explanation: Baruch Spinoza set out this principle in the opening chapter of his Tractatus Politicus, published in 1677 shortly after his death. The Dutch philosopher vowed to study human passions and behavior dispassionately, as one would study any natural phenomenon, rather than mock or condemn them.

cryptogramPlaintext: Sit down before fact as a little child, be prepared to give up every preconceived notion, follow humbly wherever and to whatever abysses nature leads, or you shall learn nothing.
explanation: The biologist Thomas Henry Huxley, nicknamed 'Darwin's Bulldog' for his fierce public defense of evolution, wrote this in a letter to the clergyman Charles Kingsley on 23 September 1860. Composed just days after the death of his young son, it captures his determination to follow evidence wherever it leads, even into uncomfortable territory.
